title: What You Cannot Touch Becomes the Brief
excerpt: The Lake Como EDITION by Neri & Hu is the most instructive hospitality opening of the year — not for what they added, but for what they refused to override. Heritage constraint is not a limitation. It is the most clarifying brief a designer can receive.
body:
## The Building Speaks First

The most interesting commission in commercial interior architecture right now is not a blank floor plate in a new tower. It is a 19th-century palazzo on Lake Como, restored by Shanghai-based Neri & Hu for EDITION Hotels, that opened this past March to sustained design press coverage — and for good reason.

What Neri & Hu did at the Lake Como EDITION deserves close reading, because the design decision that defines everything else was a refusal. In a region where virtually every hospitality conversion defaults to ornate neoclassical pastiche — gilded friezes, heavy draperies, reproduction furniture gesturing at 18th-century grandeur — they chose deliberate restraint. Pared-back material language. Clean lines. Muted tones tuned to amplify the building's existing natural light rather than compete with it.

The original high vaulting of the interior lobby was kept. The classical arches were kept. The French balconies were restored, not replaced. All the bones that define the palazzo's spatial character remained. Into that structure, Neri & Hu introduced a material palette that is contemporary without apology: a pale green marble bar anchoring the double-height entrance hall; a gridded coffered ceiling that nods to the original geometry without replicating it; Palomba stone terrazzo floors; a cantilevered teak wood entrance framed by a vertical garden of wisteria and clematis; a celadon green Sekoya marble bar in the Lobby Bar; rose-colored Dolomite plaster in the restaurant. In the guest rooms, fluted glass doors set in polished brass frames, Calacatta Turquoise marble baseboards and archways, bespoke walnut beds. The influence throughout is Carlo Scarpa — his sensual approach to material transitions, the way he made new and old speak to each other without either apologizing.

The result is a space that reads as deeply specific without being nostalgic. That is the hard thing to achieve.

## Constraint as Methodology

There is a design logic underneath this that every developer commissioning a heritage building should understand. When you cannot touch the bones, the bones set the brief. The high vault tells you how to proportion the furniture. The classical arch tells you where the threshold moment lives. The restored balcony tells you that the view is the program — which means the interior surfaces must hold attention without competing with what is outside the glass.

This is not a limitation. It is the most clarifying instruction a building can give. Projects that lack it — the blank towers, the shell-and-core commercial fits — spend the first half of the design process inventing constraints that heritage buildings already have. The discipline arrives ready-made.

We learned this at Cava Bar Centralstation in Stockholm. The Central Station building dates to 1871, a listed structure where every intervention must be reversible and every material choice must answer to what is already there. You cannot pin anything to the vaulting. You cannot alter the floor. What you discover — slowly, through documentation and argument with the preservation authorities — is that those rules do not narrow the brief, they deepen it. Every material decision carries more weight when it must perform against something that has survived 150 years. The brass detailing we chose did not need to shout; the station's existing patina spoke loudly enough. The design work became about calibration rather than invention.

## What the Press Is Missing

Most coverage of the Lake Como EDITION has focused on the celebrity chef (Mauro Colagreco, three Michelin stars, his first Italy venture), the floating pool, the biohacking spa. That is hospitality journalism doing hospitality journalism.

The more useful question is spatial: how did a contemporary interior team produce a 148-room property in a 19th-century palazzo that does not feel like a pastiche or a museum, and does not feel like a hotel that has been parachuted into a building it does not belong to? The answer is in the material transitions Neri & Hu built. The Palomba stone terrazzo into the sculptural marble staircase — a surface sequence that is new but rhymes with the palazzo's existing materiality. The fluted glass doors in polished brass frames — a detail that is contemporary in execution but Scarpa-inflected in logic, framing rather than filling. The rose-colored Dolomite plaster in the restaurant — warm, textured, absorptive, doing acoustic work as well as atmospheric work.

These are not decorative decisions. They are structural ones: decisions about how light moves, how sound behaves, how a guest's attention is directed from one space to the next. That is the level of thinking that separates a designed project from a decorated one.

## The Brief for Owners and Developers

If you are commissioning a conversion project — a listed building, a heritage industrial space, a structure with architectural memory — resist the instinct to treat the existing fabric as a problem. The building's constraints are not in the way of the design brief. They are the design brief. What cannot be touched sets the terms. The designer's job is to make the new material language answer those terms with enough specificity that the result could only exist in that building, in that place.

That specificity is not a luxury. It is what guests remember. It is what they return for.
